data.title: Barry Manilow reveals lung cancer diagnosis
data.description:
(KTLA) – Barry Manilow took to Instagram on Monday to reveal he’s been recently diagnosed with lung cancer.

The singer-songwriter said that he battled bronchitis for six weeks, only for it to return for another five weeks.

“Even though I was over the bronchitis and back on stage at the Westgate Las Vegas, my wonderful doctor ordered an MRI just to make sure that everything was OK,” the “Mandy” singer explained. “The MRI discovered a cancerous spot on my left lung that needs to be removed.”

He added, “It’s pure luck (and a great doctor) that it was found so early.”

Manilow’s doctors don’t believe the cancer has spread, and the musician is undergoing tests to confirm this diagnosis. He is set to have surgery to get the spot removed.

“So, that’s it. No chemo. No radiation. Just chicken soup and ‘I Love Lucy’ reruns,” Manilow continued. “The only follow-up is a month to recover, and that means we have to reschedule the January arena concerts.”

The artist apologized to fans but did include a list of the already rescheduled dates.

“I’m very sorry that you have to change your plans,” he said. “Just like you, we were all looking forward to the January shows and hate having to move everything around. In the meantime, I’m counting the days until I return to my home away from home at the Westgate Las Vegas for our Valentine’s weekend concerts on February 12-13-14 and throughout 2026. Something tells me that the February weekend is going to be one big party!”

The new shows will start in late February and run through late April:

  • Tampa, Benchmark International Arena – Feb. 27
  • Columbus, Nationwide Arena – March 6
  • Charleston, North Charleston Coliseum – March 11
  • Orlando, Kia Center – March 13
  • Sunrise, Amerant Bank Arena – March 14
  • Estero, Hertz Arena – March 16 & 17
  • Greensboro, First Horizon Coliseum – April 24
  • Jacksonville, VyStar Veterans Memorial – April 27
  • Duluth (GA), Gas South Arena – April 29

The “Copacabana” crooner wished his fans and followers “a wonderful Christmas and a Happy New Year” and then offered some sage health advice: “if you have even the slightest symptom… get tested!”

According to the American Cancer Society, not including skin cancer, lung cancer is the second most common cancer among men and women in the U.S.

data.title: 83K bags of frozen shrimp recalled over radioactive concerns
data.description:
(NewsNation) — The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) expanded a frozen shrimp recall including more than 83,000 bags of the raw crustaceans for potential radioactive contamination.

The shrimp, imported from Indonesia and sold nationwide under the Market 32 and Waterfront Bistro brands, may have come in contact with Cesium-137, a radioactive substance.

Stores that sold the shrimp include Price Chopper, Jewel-Osco, Albertsons, Safeway and Lucky Supermarket, the FDA said.

The shrimp was on the shelves in Colorado, Connecticut, Iowa, Idaho, Illinois, Indiana, Massachusetts, Montana, North Dakota, Nevada, New Hampshire, New York, Oregon, Pennsylvania, Utah, Wyoming and Vermont.

At Price Chopper, shrimp were sold in 1-pound bags with UPC codes of 0 41735 and 01358 3. Other stores carried 2-lb bags with codes 021130 and 13224-9.

Long-term exposure to Cesium-137 elevates an individual’s risk of cancer, according to the administration.

No illnesses have been reported as of Dec. 23, and the FDA said no U.S. products have tested positive for Cesium-137 contamination.

Customers who purchased the affected shrimp are asked to throw it away or return it to the store.

In August, the FDA issued a recall of frozen shrimp potentially contaminated by Cesium-137 from the same company, Pt. Bahari Makmur Sejati, after the radioactive substance was detected at ports in Los Angeles, Houston, Miami and Savannah, Ga.

That recall included Great Value frozen shrimp sold at Walmart.

The nation’s largest retailer was also at the center of another shrimp recall after cocktail shrimp imported from Indonesia was pulled from shelves later that same month over radioactivity concerns.

data.title: Brown University places police chief on leave amid review of deadly shooting
data.description:
Brown University placed its campus police chief on administrative leave Monday as it undergoes a comprehensive review of its security protocols and response to the Dec. 13 shooting that left two students dead and nine injured.

The school’s president, Christina Paxton, announced several actions in an email to the Brown University community Monday night, including an “After-Action Review” of the shooting, which will entail “a complete assessment and evaluation of campus safety in the period leading up to the tragedy, the preparedness and response on the date of the shooting, and the emergency management response in the aftermath.”

As the review takes place, “Vice President for Public Safety and Emergency Management Rodney Chatman will be on administrative leave, effective immediately,” the school said in a news release.

Paxton said former Providence Police Chief Hugh Clements will serve as interim vice president for public safety and chief of police, assuming day-to-day leadership of the department, as the review takes place. He will also oversee the After-Action Review and will report directly to Paxton.

“This underscores our commitment to clear accountability, leadership continuity and a sustained focus on campus safety at a time when safety is a critical part of healing and recovery for our community,” Paxton said in a news release.

In addition to commissioning an After-Action Review of the incident, Paxton announced a rapid response team is implementing security measures “with a focus on ensuring a secure campus” as much of the community leaves campus over winter break. These precautions will be in place by the start of next semester.

The school also is initiating an external comprehensive Campus Safety and Security Assessment of the university campus and nearby area “to review Brown’s current safety and security policies, procedures, infrastructure and training.”

data.title: The Movement: How strife and realignment on the right will set the tone for 2026
data.description:
From conservative personalities taking stunning swipes at each other at Turning Point USA’s AmericaFest conference to a mass exodus from the Heritage Foundation, the ideological civil war over the future of the post-Trump right has shifted into a new stage that will set the tone for 2026.

There has long been grumbling and vocal discontent from traditional conservatives about figures and institutions that have embraced fringe figures or “New Right” populism. Now, that’s escalated into litmus tests over engaging with those who espouse antisemitism and conspiracy theories — as well as structural changes in conservative institutions.

A large portion of the right does not want to have that debate. They are frustrated that the old-guard, traditional conservative wing that the MAGA movement largely repudiated is the faction defining the terms of debate. They are vehemently opposed to “cancel culture” and casting out ideological allies.

The infighting, they worry, is demoralizing the fragile President Trump coalition and harming Republican chances of keeping control of Washington in the midterms.

Those dynamics reached a new apex over the past week, with the traditionalist dissenters continuing to force the debate and making clear they will not shut up and play nice.

Ben Shapiro, as the first speaker after Charlie Kirk’s widow Erika Kirk at AmericaFest, kicked off a highly public airing of grievances — calling out “charlatans who claim to speak in the name of principle but actually traffic in conspiracism and dishonesty.” He named not only Candace Owens, who has spread conspiracy theories about Kirk’s killing, but also speakers who followed him, including Tucker Carlson, Megyn Kelly and Steve Bannon.

“The people who refused to condemn Candace’s truly vicious attacks — and some of them are speaking here tonight — are guilty of cowardice,” Shapiro said. He referenced Carlson’s October interview with antisemetic commentator Nick Fuentes saying those who host “a Hitler apologist, Nazi-loving” commentator like Fuentes “ought to own it.”

Carlson, Kelly and Bannon all later responded to Shapiro on the main stage. Carlson said he laughed at Shapiro making “calls to deplatform at a Charlie Kirk event.” Bannon said that Shapiro “is like a cancer, and a cancer that spreads.”

Kelly, who helped set up a meeting between Owens and Erika Kirk last week, said she no longer considers Shapiro a friend and said that while she does not agree with Owens’s theories about Israel and more, she didn’t call her out for asking questions “because I favored her asking them.”

Meanwhile, the Heritage Foundation faced a major staff and board member exodus that showcased not only infighting between podcast personalities but how major money and structural shifts are changing the institutional structures on the right.

Heritage is another organization that has been at the center of the conservative civil war, stemming partly from its President Kevin Roberts in October defending Carlson for interviewing Fuentes, which Roberts later apologized for.

But staff discontent had been growing long before that issue as Roberts led Heritage in a Trumpier and more populist direction, embracing more noninterventionist foreign policy and warming up to tariffs that it previously opposed.

First, two more board members resigned last week — Shane McCullar and Abby Spencer Moffat, whose family had supported Heritage for decades and made the largest pledge to the organization in its history. McCullar said the board was “unwilling to confront the lapses in judgment that have harmed its credibility,” and Moffat said the organization had drifted “from the principles that once defined its leadership.” A third board member, Robert P. George, had resigned from Heritage a month prior.

Then, nearly all of the staff from Heritage’s legal and economic centers abruptly departed the think tank, Roberts told staff in a late Sunday email obtained by The Hill. Heritage said two of the staffers were fired for “conduct inconsistent with Heritage’s mission and standards,” mentioning “fiduciary duty and intellectual property removal.” 

On Monday, Advancing American Freedom (AAF), the think tank founded by former Vice President Mike Pence, announced it was absorbing more than a dozen staff members who were decamping from Heritage — including three directors.

The backstory shows how this is a major structural shift in the conservative movement — and that money talks just as much as those snarky, swipe-y podcasters.

Talks to absorb the Meese legal center team started several weeks ago, AAF President Tim Chapman said. The AAF board approved the plans on the condition it raised at least 60 percent of a $15 million fundraising goal, enough to fund the new project for $5 million a year for three years. After approaching a small number of donors, the AAF raised $12 million in just two weeks. 

With the additions, the 4-year-old think tank is doubling in size and is already working to expand its downtown Washington, D.C., office space. More staff additions are expected. (More on all that in my story from Monday: Heritage Foundation staffers decamp for Pence-founded think tank in latest exodus)

Chapman said the changes reflect “the reorganizing of the right” — and that the personality clashes at AmericaFest, while they were based on somewhat different disputes over ideological purity and who is part of the conservative movement, are all showing the same thing. 

“I think that there’s a core of the conservative movement that is still engaged in principled conservatism and still wants to fight for that stuff,” Chapman said. 

“The advantage that we have is that the truth is on our side,” Chapman said. “Facts are really stubborn things, and there’s a lot of noise and a lot of chaos around what you see online. But when a guy like Ben Shapiro stands up and just tells it straight and it’s completely irrefutable, that kind of stands on its own. We’re going to do similar things here.”

“When it comes to public policy analysis, our analysis will be rigorous, it will be fact-based, it will be true, and people can disagree with it as a political matter, but they won’t be able to disagree with it as a factual matter,” Chapman said.

Trump allies, meanwhile, dismissed the shifts.

“Congrats to @Heritage ! The clowns are leaving their organization,” posted MAGA strategist Alex Bruesewitz. “These departures will also open jobs up for patriots who believe in America First!”

Donald Trump Jr. said in a post on social platform X: “I think it’s great news for Heritage that a bunch of Trump-hating RINOs are leaving. Anyone who would want to go work for Mike Pence’s globalist never-Trump organization isn’t MAGA and definitely doesn’t put America First!” 

The midterm elections — and the Republican primaries before it — will be not only a major test of Trump’s popularity and power in Washington, but also of which ideological factions will keep or gain dominance heading into 2028 and a post-Trump landscape.

Will the MAGA coalition endure, or will the traditionalists see a resurgence? I’ll be in your inbox every week following all of it with you in the new year.

Further reading … Audrey Fahlberg in National Review with the scoop on Heritage departures … Wall Street Journal: Top Heritage Officials Flee to Mike Pence’s Nonprofit as Think Tank Fractures … Politico: MAGA infighting erupts at Turning Point USA Conference … New York Times: Turning Point’s Annual Gathering Turns Into a Gripefest

Welcome to The Movement, a weekly newsletter looking at the influences and debates on the right in Washington. I’m Emily Brooks, House leadership reporter at The Hill. 

Programming note: The Movement will not publish next week. I’ll be back in your inbox on Tuesday, Jan. 6. Until then, have a Merry Christmas and a Happy New Year!

Send me tips, comments and suggestions: ebrooks@thehill.com.

Follow me on X: @emilybrooksnews

Not already on the list? Subscribe here to get The Movement in your inbox Tuesday mornings. 

GOP DIVISION ON TRUMP’S MARIJUANA MOVE

President Trump’s move last week to expedite rescheduling marijuana is clashing with the growing sentiment opposed to the drug among conservatives, leading to notable pushback from Republicans. 

There had seemed to be a growing sense in the conservative and MAGA base that state legalizations of marijuana was the wrong move, and normalization of the drug had led to an epidemic of unmotivated young people who preferred playing video games. A Gallup survey found a sharp decline in support for legalization of marijuana over the last year, from 53 percent in 2024 to 40 percent in October 2025.

The stated goal of moving the drug from Schedule I to Schedule III, akin to ketamine, was to expand cannabis research.

But Republicans and conservatives have balked at the move.

Nearly half the Senate Republican Conference signed a letter led by Sen. Ted Budd (R-N.C.) that warned rescheduling the drug could pose health and economic threats, my colleague Alex Bolton reported.

“Marijuana has long-term effects on brain health, including ‘permanent IQ loss’ when a person starts using marijuana at a young age,” they wrote, adding, “We cannot reindustrialize America if we encourage marijuana use. America’s workplace and America’s roads are endangered by marijuana use.”

Over in the House, Reps. Pete Sessions (R-Texas) and Andy Harris (R-Md.), the chair of the House Freedom Caucus, led a letter of 26 GOP colleagues urging Trump to reconsider rescheduling.

“Reclassifying marijuana as a Schedule III drug will send the wrong message to America’s children, enable drug cartels, and make our roads more dangerous,” they wrote, adding, “The marijuana industry wants Schedule III because large marijuana companies will receive billions in tax relief. … Tax relief should be prioritized for hard-working, law-abiding Americans and businesses, not marijuana shops.”

The Wall Street Journal editorial board, meanwhile, said that the move “sends the wrong message to vulnerable young brains,” sending the signal “that marijuana isn’t all that harmful, despite mounting evidence that it is.”

ON MY CALENDAR

It’s a quiet few weeks ahead for the holidays.

Have an event I should feature here in January? Flag it for me: ebrooks@thehill.com

THREE MORE THINGS

  1. A day before his America Fest speech, Ben Shapiro gave a speech at the Heritage Foundation — with an introduction from Kevin Roberts —that featured some rather pointed sentiments, given the setting, and an expansive repudiation of Tucker Carlson.

    “The conservative movement is in flux. It’s in flux because of the systemic failure by conservative leaders to do what any good leaders must do to find and maintain the foundations of that movement,” Shapiro said. “If, as Heritage Foundation proclaims, our goal ought to be to formulate and promote conservative public policies based on the principles of free enterprise, limited government, individual freedom, traditional American values, and a strong national defense, then  we must stand up against those who would pervert and twist the conservative movement into a movement without principles, or worse, a movement that promotes the very opposite of the principles that conservatives hold dear.”
  2. Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) said at America Fest that he will pursue putting a statue of Charlie Kirk in the Capitol building. Referencing the push from Rep. Andy Biggs (R-Ariz.) to display such a statute, Johnson said: “Yes, Andy Biggs, we’re going to work on that statute in the Capitol. Okay?” The crowd responded with cheers.
  3. Vice President Vance, who told UnHerd that it is “absurd” to say that Tucker Carlson has no place in the conservative movement, was the far-and-away leader in the America Fest 2028 straw poll, getting 84.4 percent support. Secretary of State Marco Rubio came in second with 4.8 percent. Another interesting result, per Charlie Kirk Show Producer Blake Neff: Nearly 90 percent supported an immigration moratorium.

WHAT I’M READING

  • CNN’s Steve Contorno: Turning Point exposed a brewing MAGA civil war. Can JD Vance unite it?
  • Talk of the town. Jacob Savace in Compact Magazine: The Lost Generation
  • …and a response. New York Times’s Ross Douthat: There’s a Simpler Explanation for the Rightward Shift of Young Men
  • Ben Jacobs in New York Magazine: Why Rep. Stefanik Is Leaving Politics

data.title: Are you a ‘principled’ conservative?
data.description:
The number of people claiming to be conservatives has increased over the past few years, though only a portion of them are “principled conservatives.” That distinction is important, because a person who isn’t a principled conservative likely isn’t a conservative at all. 

A defining characteristic of conservatism is an embrace of certain time-tested principles. In 1960, 100 young conservatives convened at National Review editor William F. Buckley’s home in Sharon, Conn., to draft a set of 12 principles, referred to as the “Sharon Statement,” which defined the post-war conservative movement. Recently, the Principles First group and the Freedom Conservatives adopted a similar set of principles, as has Our Republican Legacy. 

Liberals and progressives, by contrast, adopt goals instead of principles, such as striving for a government-run health care system, universal basic income, and a unionized workforce. For them, principles are obstacles to their goals. The ends justify the means. 

To be a principled conservative, one needs to generally agree with the basic principles that have defined a conservative worldview for decades and stand by those principles even if doing so doesn’t lead to a preferred outcome.  

Let’s start with one of the key principles for conservatives: faithful adherence to the U.S. Constitution. Principled conservatives believe the Constitution is the basis for the government’s structure and powers. When the Constitution speaks clearly on an issue, the principled conservative accepts it even if he or she disagrees — or seeks to amend the document, thereby establishing a new principle. For example, occasionally a conservative judge or Supreme Court justice will render a decision while stating he doesn’t necessarily like the decision. But he’s following the law and not his personal preferences.  

Liberals and progressives, on the other hand, often speak of a “living Constitution” whose guidelines can change with the times and public opinion, giving liberal judges the freedom to render the decision they want to give rather than what the Constitution clearly provides.  

The Constitution empowers Congress to make laws, impose taxes and spend money. When President Joe Biden decided to bypass Congress and forgive more than $400 billion in student loan debt, conservatives rightly denounced the plan — and the Supreme Court stopped him. On the other hand, some conservatives have remained silent, and even defended, President Donald Trump’s bypassing Congress and imposing tariffs (taxes) on any country for any reason or for no reason.  

Another long-held conservative principle is fiscal responsibility. With $38.5 trillion in federal debt and annual budget deficits projected to run in the $1.7-to-$1.5 trillion range for the next decade, there has never been a better time for conservatives to demand fiscal responsibility. And yet many self-identifying conservatives are saying almost nothing about government’s current spending spree, even supporting the president’s proposal of handing out $2,000 to most Americans.  

A third conservative principle: free enterprise. A dictionary definition of free enterprise states, “an economic system in which private business operates in competition and largely free of state control.” Remember when Presidents George W. Bush, a Republican, and Barack Obama, a Democrat, decided to bailout GM and Chrysler, eventually spending $81 billion bailing out the auto industry. Many conservatives criticized the steps. Refusing to help the companies might mean GM and Chrysler would fail, but government in a free enterprise system isn’t supposed to bailout struggling companies.  

Of course, Biden ladled out money to the auto industry, green energy companies, tech companies and many others — always with government-imposed strings attached. And conservatives rightly criticized the Biden administration for picking winners and losers with taxpayer money.  

However, President Trump has secured veto power over U.S. Steel’s business decisions and is playing an increasing role in many other companies. As the Tax Policy Center writes, “Trump has made no secret of his desire to greatly expand the government’s ability and his personal authority to manage investment decisions normally made by business. This goes well beyond broad policy initiatives to encourage domestic manufacturing.” And yet many of the conservatives surrounding the president praise his business interventions, even when companies aren’t financially struggling. 

All of this raises the issue of hypocrisy. The time is coming, perhaps as soon as 2029, when a Democrat will be in the White House again. That person will almost surely follow Trump’s lead in expanding the role of government in business, doling out taxpayer money, imposing tariffs or other taxes at will, and ignoring constitutional restraints when inconvenient — and do much or all of it by executive order, bypassing Congress. Conservatives will certainly denounce any Democratic president for doing so.  

But that raises the question: If a person is only a strong defender of conservative principles when the other party is in power, is that individual really a principled conservative?  

Merrill Matthews is the co-author of “On the Edge: America Faces the Entitlements Cliff.”

data.title: The MAHA base is in danger of fracturing as its agenda flails
data.description:
Defenders of the slow pace of progress at Health and Human Services — especially on vaccines — argue that Health Secretary Robert Kennedy is not a king. He works for President Trump and has certain limitations. He is doing the best he can considering the circumstances. We must be happy with the incremental progress, they say.

But Trump himself recently blew a hole is this narrative by ordering Secretary Kennedy to review the childhood vaccine schedule and to bring it in line with that of other developed countries. For example, Denmark only requires a total of 22 doses, compared to 72 in the U.S.

So the question remains, what is holding HHS back from truly reforming the childhood vaccine schedule?

It started after the election. Kennedy was appointed to the transition team after his historic endorsement of Trump. Given the trepidation about Trump among many in Kennedy’s Make-America-Healthy-Again base, this was reassuring news. This meant he would be taking a role in policy and personnel decisions, not only at the health agencies, but across the government. Given Trump’s questionable personnel decisions in the first administration (thanks to a compromised transition team), this came as a relief.

Unfortunately, however, Kennedy did not have the influence we had hoped. He appointed Dr. James Lyons-Weiler to run the HHS transition on his behalf. Weiler interviewed over 150 candidates in November and December, but he did so without any coordination with the personnel operation at Mar-a-Lago. The result was that few of Weiler’s candidates were hired.

This is why you do not see medical freedom heroes like Doctors Harvey Risch, Ryan Cole or Pierre Korey at HHS — although Risch was just given the consolation prize of being named chair of the President’s Cancer Panel.

Personnel is policy, and this is the number one reason the MAHA agenda has been stymied. 

Today, less than a year after that historic appointment and Kennedy’s against-all-odds confirmation in the teeth of intense opposition by the U.S. medical and pharmaceutical lobbies, MAHA is in grave danger of stalling. Not only are tens of thousands of federal bureaucrats working to subvert the agenda, but competing forces and interests in the Trump administration are also seeking to undermine it at every opportunity.

Scores of the most qualified MAHA candidates for political appointment to HHS and other health agencies were screened out for political reasons, while establishment Republicans indifferent to the movement are driving HHS policy. 

Secretary Kennedy himself seems strangely constrained in his actions and words, and many parts of his signature agenda are going nowhere. The people accomplishing the most for the movement — such as Dr. Steven Hatfill, who canceled $500 million in mRNA research after meticulously providing receipts of the dangers and negative efficacy of the platform — have been fired under false pretenses. The ineffective COVID vaccine, which MAHA supporters believe has caused millions of injuries and deaths, is still on the market and, at least for immunocompromised children, remains part of the childhood vaccine schedule.

Communication and coalition-building by Kennedy’s team has been abysmal, with no attempt to disseminate talking points or utilize surrogates on major MAHA initiatives. This is resulting in public confusion and an exclusively negative media narrative that goes unopposed and causes enormous political damage to the cause. For example, Kennedy was forced to walk back the HHS assertion that there is a link between Tylenol and autism, telling reporters there is “not sufficient” evidence to prove Tylenol “definitively causes” autism. There does not seem to be any strategic communications plan whatsoever.

Many in the movement are demoralized, abandoning hope that this administration can achieve any reforms that will tame the Big Pharma beast and hold the medical establishment accountable, while shifting the incentives favoring corruption and chronic disease in a better direction. The window is closing, the historic coalition in danger of being scattered.

Up to this point, the MAHA base has been willing to grant Kennedy the benefit of the doubt to navigate the treacherous political waters he inhabits. But the natives are getting restless. While movement stalwarts cheer on small accomplishments — such as the banning of dangerous food dyes, the voluntary removal of seed oils from processed food, the dismissal of previous members of the interest-conflicted Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices, and the recent advisory on Tylenol and autism — their patience is wearing thin because of stalled progress on central parts of the MAHA agenda.

Missteps have caused many in the MAHA movement to give up on Trump and Kennedy. This includes the precipitous approval of the Moderna SPIKEVAXX COVID vaccine this summer, the firing of Hatfill, and the humiliating Oval Office press conference in which Trump praised Pfizer CEO Albert Bourla for doing a terrific job during the COVID pandemic. Trump also allowed Bourla’s company to avoid proposed taxes in return for a promise to reshore production of Pfizer’s drugs and vaccines.

We know Kennedy is a true believer. Contrary to what many of his MAHA critics think, he has not sold out. But he is under tremendous pressure from outside forces. As Lauren Lee has said, outside pressure, if constructive, is helpful to the Kennedy and his agenda. But the White House is balancing pharma against the MAHA base. The more we are heard and the more we flex our muscles, the more cover it gives Kennedy. 

Gray Delany is the owner of Calamo Press and is the former Director of MAHA Implementation at HHS.

data.title: ‘America First’ agenda must include tackling the debt crisis
data.description:
With the controversial Epstein vote in the rearview mirror, it is time for Congress to focus on the debt crisis — to avoid continued inflation and the prospect of a meltdown of the U.S. economy. The debt crisis is the premier national security issue of our time.

An America First policy would focus Congress on our nation’s debt crisis. Our nation is on track to spend over $2 trillion more than it took in this year. Using 2024 numbers, our federal government spent $6.8 trillion while collecting $4.9 trillion primarily from individual income taxes and payroll taxes.

The delta between what our government takes in versus what it spends is unsustainable. While the left would love to use hike taxes to continue massive government spending, there are some America First politicians who want to bring the federal government into spending levels consistent with the limited government ideals promoted by our Founding Fathers.

Members of both parties in Congress have fallen flat on their face dealing with the debt crisis. Americans concerned about inflation should be outraged at both parties, because debt causes inflation indirectly. As the federal government borrows money and expands the money supply, the dollar is devalued. The Federal Reserve deserves some blame, because that institution has not done a great job of keeping the money supply tight. Fed reform should also be on the agenda, because that institution deserves a good deal of blame for our debt crisis.

Congress will continue in January to battle over an appropriations bill for the current fiscal year that will saddle Americans with a new $2 trillion in debt by September 30, 2026. Democrats fought the Republican bill, but they did so for the wrong reasons. Democrats just want higher spending on discretionary accounts alongside higher levels of government spending on health care.

Congress should embrace efforts by my former boss, Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.), to cut the size and growth of government. Back in 2016, he proposed the “Penny Plan,” which would have spent one penny less each year for five years for every on-budget dollar the federal government was spending in fiscal 2018, excepting Social Security. Back in 2016, the national debt was $19.6 trillion. Today the national debt is nearly twice that — $38.2 trillion.

Our nation is experiencing an insane amount of government spending, and politicians have not taken significant action to control it. Paul has had to change his five penny plan from 2016 into a “Six Penny Plan” because of an accelerated debt crisis. Paul put out a press release in September pointing out that “interest payments on the debt now surpass the America’s military spending, underscoring how runaway borrowing is crowding out core national priorities. With the national debt exceeding $37 trillion, it now stands at nearly twice the value of all bank deposits in the country.”

In other words, even if all private bank accounts were seized by Uncle Sam, our federal government would only be able pay down half the debt it owes.

When the Senate had an opportunity to cut spending, it failed. Paul forced a vote on his Six Penny Plan and had the support of 36 Republicans — a good mix of back benchers and leaders such as Sen. John Thune (R-S.D.).

The Trump Administration has taken some important steps in tackling spending, but it can only do so much on its own. Elon Musk’s Department of Government Efficiency and the Office of Management and Budget sent recissions packages to Congress to cancel previously appropriated and wasteful spending. Congress, however, has not taken appropriate action to do its part.

It might be time for Congress to take a hard look at a Balanced Budget Amendment to the Constitution. A good amendment would require a balanced budget for each fiscal year while also limiting federal spending no more than 18 percent of GDP. A good amendment would require a supermajority (two-thirds elected and sworn) to hike taxes, raise the debt limit or run a debt during declared war. In addition, the amendment must bar courts from ordering tax hikes to balance the budget. This would add teeth to the idea that we need to get control of government spending.

The America First movement needs leaders like Paul and Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) fighting to restrain the ever-expanding scope of federal spending and power over the lives of average Americans.

Brian Darling is former counsel for Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.).

data.title: Republicans split on Trump’s Venezuela endgame
data.description:
Morning Report is The Hill’s a.m. newsletter. Subscribe here.

In today’s issue:

▪ GOP skeptical on Venezuela goals

▪ Second batch of Epstein files released

▪ Trump unveils new warships named after himself

▪ Weiss defends pulling ‘60 Minutes’ segment

Republicans are divided on whether President Trump should increase military pressure to oust Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro, with some pointing to the U.S.’s poor experience with regime change in recent decades.

Trump has gradually ramped up pressure on Maduro, whom the administration has accused of facilitating drug smuggling into the U.S., conducting strikes on alleged drug-trafficking boats in the Caribbean and deploying warships, surveillance planes, fighter aircraft and an aircraft carrier to the region.

Most recently, the administration has begun seizing oil tankers that it says are carrying sanctioned oil as Trump declared a blockade on all such vessels entering or exiting Venezuela.

The president hasn’t made clear what his ultimate goal is regarding Venezuela but has refused to rule out any options. He has repeatedly signaled he would launch land strikes targeting drug production facilities soon and would possibly even send troops into the country.

Trump also has said he believes Maduro’s “days are numbered.” He told NBC News last week he wouldn’t rule out warwith Venezuela and that Maduro “knows exactly what I want,” declining to elaborate.

The commander-in-chief’s approach has struck at a divide within the GOP between a more traditional faction supporting a hawkish foreign policy and another camp supporting noninterventionist policies.

The Hill’s Alexander Bolton reports Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told GOP senators last week that Trump isn’t pursuing regime change, but some Republicans are skeptical given White House chief of staff Susie Wiles’s comments to Vanity Fair in which she said Trump wants to keep “blowing up boats until Maduro cries uncle.”

Senate Republicans largely support Trump’s tactics of targeting Venezuelan boats, but some argue attacking the regime directly would go too far.

“I think we just have to be very careful when we’re dealing with regime change. It seems to backfire a lot,” Kansas Sen. Roger Marshall (R) said.

Another Republican senator said they believe the administration is intent on ousting Maduro.

“I don’t want to have another Iraq or Afghanistan,” they said, referring to the wars in which the U.S. helped oust Saddam Hussein and the Taliban from power.

Both situations quickly turned into quagmires that dragged on for years.

Trump said Monday that it would be “smart” for Maduro to give up power, but said it’s up to him.

“But again, we’re going to find out,” he said.

Meanwhile, Trump on Monday continued to ramp up his rhetoric against Colombian President Gustavo Petro, warning him to “watch his a‑‑” and close alleged drug factories in his country.

“I love the Colombian people,” Trump said. “But their new leader is a troublemaker, and he better watch it. He better close up those cocaine factories. There are at least three major cocaine factories. We know where they are.”

▪ The Hill: Trump says U.S. will keep oil and ships seized near Venezuela.

▪ The Hill: China slams seizure of Venezuelan oil tankers.

▪ The Hill: U.S. carries out another strike in the East Pacific.

PRESIDENT UNVEILS ‘TRUMP-CLASS’ WARSHIPS: Trump has one more major item bearing his name –– a new class of battleships, he announced Monday.

Trump said he approved a plan for the Navy to start constructing two battleships as part of the “Golden Fleet,” which the Navy said last week would protect larger ships from surface vessels and fighter jets. The ships will be equipped with guns, missiles, hypersonic weapons and high-powered lasers.

The president said the ships will take about two and a half years to build. The military is expected to have between 20 and 25 of them.

The ships are the latest item set to bear the president’s name. The administration has pushed to brand other buildings and initiatives after the president, including the new “Trump Accounts” for kids, the U.S. Institute of Peace and the Kennedy Center.

3 Things to Know Today

1. The Food and Drug Administration has approved an oral version of the weight-loss drug Wegovy, the first such supplement approved for public use. Novo Nordisk expects to launch the pill early next year.

2. The Trump administration has ordered almost 30 ambassadors to return to the U.S. within weeks, The New York Times reports. All were appointed and confirmed during the Biden administration.

3. U.S. District Judge Aileen Cannon ordered Jack Smith’s report on Trump’s handling of classified and sensitive documents to remain private for now, giving the president 60 days to challenge its publication.

Leading the Day

EPSTEIN FILES DROP: The Department of Justice (DOJ) early Tuesday released thousands of additional documents it has on Jeffrey Epstein, following up on its first release from the end of last week.

CBS News reported the latest drop includes more than 11,000 files, including multi-paged documents and hundreds of videos. It also includes court records, FBI and DOJ documents, emails and news clippings.

Many of the documents include references to Trump, including one that shows a subpoena was sent to Mar-a-Lago in 2021 for records related to the government’s case against Epstein accomplice Ghislaine Maxwell, The Washington Post reported. They also include notes from an assistant U.S. attorney in New York about the number of times Trump flew on Epstein’s plane.

Being named in the Epstein files is not itself considered an accusation of wrongdoing, and Trump has maintained that he broke off his ties with Epstein long before the convicted sex offender’s arrest and death in 2019.

Trump pushed back on Monday over photos released on people who he said “innocently met” with Epstein. He called the release of photos showing former President Clinton with Epstein a “terrible thing.”

“I don’t like the pictures of Bill Clinton being shown. I don’t like the pictures of other people being shown. I think it’s a terrible thing,” he said.

▪ The Hill: Former Clinton spokesperson calls for full release of files.

▪ The Hill: Dems acknowledge Clinton’s ties to Epstein don’t look good.

Meanwhile, lawmakers are wrestling with what their next steps should be on the Epstein files.

The Hill’s Sudiksha Kochi and Rebecca Beitsch report that members of Congress are divided on how strongly to push back against the DOJ for failing to share all the unclassified documents it has on the disgraced financier.

Reps. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) and Ro Khanna (D-Calif.), who led the effort to require the files’ release, have said they’re drafting a resolution to hold Attorney General Pam Bondi in “inherent contempt,” fining her every day the remaining files aren’t released. Other Democrats have called for examining all legal options. But some have raised doubts about doing so immediately.

WEISS BACKLASH: Concerns are spiking among critics of CBS News Editor-in-Chief Bari Weiss about the independence of the outlet following her decision to pull a “60 Minutes” segment shortly before it was to air.

The segment was set to cover poor conditions inside a notorious prison in El Salvador, known as CECOT, where the Trump administration has sent Venezuelan migrants. But Weiss argued the segment wasn’t ready for publication yet and had it pulled a few hours before Sunday’s episode.

Weiss reportedly requested several changes to the segment, arguing it also needed an on-the-record statement from the administration. But the correspondent who covered the story, Sharyn Alfonsi, alleged in a note to her colleagues obtained by multiple outlets that the decision was “political.”

Alfonsi said she reached out to the White House, State Department and Department of Homeland Security but didn’t receive a response. She maintained that the story had been screened five times and had been cleared to air.

“Government silence is a statement, not a VETO. Their refusal to be interviewed is a tactical maneuver designed to kill the story,” she said in the email to her colleagues. “If the administration’s refusal to participate becomes a valid reason to spike a story, we have effectively handed them a ‘kill switch’ for any reporting they find inconvenient.”

The decision to pull the segment marks one of the first major controversies for Weiss since she was tapped to lead the news outlet in October. She was appointed chief editor after CBS’s parent company, Paramount, acquired her media startup company, The Free Press.

Weiss has been a strong critic of mainstream media coverage, and supporters and critics alike have widely expected shake-ups at CBS under her leadership.

Questions about media independence and journalistic integrity arose earlier following Paramount’s $16 million settlementwith Trump over an interview with former Vice President Kamala Harris on “60 Minutes” that aired last year and the administration’s subsequent approval of a merger between Paramount and Skydance.

Lawmakers critical of Trump and his allies slammed Weiss’s decision, arguing it demonstrates the president is successfully intimidating news outlets.

“What is happening to CBS is a terrible embarrassment and if executives think they can build shareholder value by avoiding journalism that might offend the Mad King they are about to learn a tough lesson,” Sen. Brian Schatz (D-Hawaii) wrote on the social platform X. “This is still America and we don’t enjoy bulls— like this.”

Weiss is standing by her decision, telling The New York Times in a statement that holding stories that aren’t ready happens every day in newsrooms. She has said the segment will air once it’s ready.

▪ The Hill: What to know about Weiss’s decision.

▪ CNBC: Larry Ellison backing Paramount’s bid for Warner Bros. Discovery.

WIND PROJECTS SUSPENDED: Senate Democrats are walking away from talks about permitting reform following the Trump administration’s announcement that it’s suspending five major offshore wind projects.

Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said on Monday that the leases were being paused because of “national security concerns.” A press release from the department cited potential radar interference known as “clutter” from wind turbines, arguing it obscures “legitimate moving targets and generates false targets” near the wind projects.

All five projects are located on the East Coast, near New England, New York and Virginia. The projects together would be expected to generate enough electricity to power 2.5 million homes, according to the projects’ websites.

Democratic Sens. Sheldon Whitehouse (R.I.) and Martin Heinrich (N.M.) said they will abandon long-running discussions about legislation to speed up energy and infrastructure construction unless the administration reverses course. They’ve been negotiating with Republican Sens. Mike Lee (Utah) and Shelley Moore Capito (W.Va.) on a possible deal for months.

“The illegal attacks on fully permitted renewable energy projects must be reversed if there is to be any chance that permitting talks resume. There is no path to permitting reform if this administration refuses to follow the law,” Heinrich and Whitehouse said in a joint statement.

▪ Reuters: Shares of offshore wind projects drop after decision.

▪ The Hill: Trump rejects Colorado disaster funding for wildfires, flooding.

FOREIGN DRONE BAN: The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) has banned new foreign-made drones from being sold in the U.S., citing national security concerns.

The order prevents these products from being sold or imported in the U.S. but doesn’t apply to technology that’s already been sold in the country. The FCC made the decision in line with the 2025 National Defense Authorization Act, which called for reviewing the national security risks that certain technologies pose.

DJI, a Chinese company, holds a majority of the drone industry’s market share.

When & Where

The president is in Florida and has no public events scheduled.

The Senate will meet at 8:30 a.m. for a pro forma session.

The House will convene at noon for a pro forma session.

Zoom In

REVVING UP: The shadow primary for the 2028 Republican presidential race is showing signs of getting underway, with potential candidates’ names gaining more attention.

Erika Kirk, who took over as head of the influential conservative group Turning Point USA following the assassination of her husband, made a splash last week when she backed Vice President Vance for the GOP’s 2028 presidential nomination. Vance hasn’t publicly said if he will run but has been seen as the de facto front-runner in the Republican race to become Trump’s successor.

But The Hill’s Julia Manchester reports that some cracks have formed within the conservative movement over who should take the reins from Trump after his term ends.

Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.) signaled he wouldn’t back Vance, arguing the party has gotten away from its traditional values of free trade and limited government.

Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Texas) has recently been reported to be considering another presidential run, enthused by a split with Vance on foreign policy.

Rubio has also been seen as a possible heir apparent to Trump, who has praised his secretary of State and suggested he might run with Vance on a ticket.

Other names that have generated buzz include Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis (R) and Trump’s son, Donald Trump Jr.

While Vance may be the early favorite, signs point to a competitive race for the GOP following next year’s midterms.

▪ The Hill: Republican says Vance, Rubio more likely to run together.

▪ CNN: Half say they’re already thinking about 2028.

Elsewhere

HERITAGE EXODUS: More than a dozen staff members have left the Heritage Foundation and joined former Vice President Mike Pence’s Advancing American Freedom (AAF).

The Hill’s Emily Brooks reports Heritage Foundation President Kevin Roberts announced the departures internally in an all-staff email on Sunday, and AAF announced the hirings Monday.

Heritage lost most of its staff from the group’s legal and economic centers, while AAF is nearly doubling in size.

“We wish them well, though the manner of their departures speaks volumes,” Roberts said in the email obtained by The Hill.

The departures are the latest blow in the fallout stemming from Roberts’s defense of Tucker Carlson for interviewing white nationalist Nick Fuentes. Roberts later apologized, but staff had been growing dissatisfied long before the incident as he has led the think tank in a more pro-Trump, populist and noninterventionist direction.

Check out more of Brooks’s reporting in her weekly newsletter, The Movement. Click here to sign up.

▪ The Wall Street Journal: Top Heritage officials flee.

CONWAY IN: Lawyer and staunch Trump critic George Conway has joined the increasingly crowded Democratic primary race to succeed Rep. Jerry Nadler (D-N.Y.).

A filing from the Federal Election Commission shows Conway, who co-founded the anti-Trump Lincoln Project after being a lifelong Republican, filed his candidacy on Monday. Conway had been married to former Trump adviser Kellyanne Conway before they divorced in 2023.

George Conway left the Republican Party during Trump’s first term and has consistently stoked the president’s ire since then.

The New York Times reported last month that Conway was looking at a run. He’s set to join a bustling Democratic field in New York’s solidly blue 12th Congressional District.

Other candidates include New York State Assembly members Alex Bores and Micah Lasher; Parkland, Fla., shooting survivor and gun control advocate Cameron Kasky; and political commentator Jack Schlossberg, the grandson of former President John F. Kennedy.

▪ The Independent: Trump’s former pollster finds Dems lead on generic ballot.

Opinion

A challenge for 2026: Make yourself a little uncomfortable, The Hill’s Chris Stirewalt writes.

This is the year millennials officially got old, Anna Silman writes in The New York Times.

The Closer

And finally … The Kansas City Chiefs appear set to move across state lines in the 2030s as the team announced Monday it reached a deal to move to Kansas from its current home in Missouri.

A council of top lawmakers in Kansas voted unanimously at the state Capitol to allow the state to use more than $2.4 billion in bonds to cover 60 percent of the cost of a new, domed stadium; a new training facility; and retail and entertainment space, The Associated Press reported.

The bonds will be paid off with state sales and liquor tax revenue generated in the area around it.

The stadium is expected to be built in Kansas City, Kan., in an area near the home of the Major League Soccer team, Sporting Kansas City. It’s expected to be ready to open before the 2031 season.

“The location of Chiefs games will change, but some things won’t change,” Chiefs owner Clark Hunt reportedly said after the meeting. “Our fans will still be the loudest in the NFL, our games will still be the best place in the world to tailgate, and our players and coaches will be ready to compete for championships, because on the field or off the field, we are big dreamers, and we’re ready for the next chapter.”

The Chiefs are coming toward the end of their first losing season and first time missing the playoffs in more than a decade after multiple Super Bowl wins.

data.title: A challenge for 2026: Make yourself a little uncomfortable
data.description:
For a short time in my life, part of every day’s work was to call the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers to get the level of the Ohio River at the Pike Island Locks and Dam. Now, even a basic AI model can get that kind of information, publish it and offer a brief paragraph on what bargemen and boaters can expect and on relative flood risks.

Artificial intelligence is proving to be, as most things in life, a mixed blessing for the news business. Jobs continue to be lost, particularly the entry-level positions like the ones I had 30 years ago that were basically transcription gigs: government meetings, sports scores, police reports, obituaries, etc. 

It’s bad that those minimum-wage positions that let someone like me get into the business continue to disappear. It’s good that the work may actually still get done. America lost something like 26,000 newsroom jobs between 2008 and 2019, which followed a period of even more brutal contraction in the previous decade when online advertising shattered the industry and led to a purge of as many as 50,000 newsroom jobs. 

The brunt of that fell on small and midsize markets, where the work of basic newsgathering — and therefore basic accountability for people in powerful positions in these places — often almost completely died out.      

Artificial intelligence is now bringing some of that back, allowing hollowed-out newsrooms to restore coverage and lowering startup costs for new outlets. Unlike many sectors now being gutted by AI, the news business already had its catastrophe. News was among the first losers, so now it is among the first winners. That’s very good. And, in the hands of an experienced journalist, these data tools can help with projects that would have been infeasible even in the heyday of the business.  

And there’s the rub. Transparency and accountability are two related but very different things. Information has never been cheaper or more plentiful, but the things that matter are very likely to be lost in this digital ocean if they are not outright destroyed by the false or facile. The difference between what’s available and what’s useful are people who know where to look, which questions to ask and what’s important. 

And what’s most important — indeed the principal reason for protecting the existence of a noisy, quarrelsome, factious, muckraking free press in our republic — is accountability. A free press, however annoying, is an essential check on power.   

But the cheap information and low barriers to entry that may help underserved markets also means that where there’s already a crowd, especially in big cities and for national news, there is a race to the bottom. In those markets that are oversupplied, particularly for national political news, the digital glut tends to drive quality down further.

The incentives for those trying to build or retain a small share of a big, highly competitive market can be good if the winning advantages are about price, preference and quality. That’s great for most consumer products. But for news and information, it’s different. With an advertising-based model, consumer costs are usually zero for a product that is available everywhere, all the time, so preferences matter more. And it turns out that in news, what people tend to prefer is what they already like.

We see how that works in entertainment. What consumers tend to want to watch or listen to is what they’ve seen or heard before — or products that are very similar. Of the 10 most-watched streaming shows of 2025, according to Nielsen, only three — “Bluey,”  “The Rookie” and “Squid Game” — are from franchises less than 10 years old. The rest are all reruns or rewarmed versions of the originals. 

If you take comfort in rewatching “The Big Bang Theory” or enjoy turning your brain off to let “Grey’s Anatomy” or “NCIS” distract you with a familiar cast in predictable storylines, there’s no harm. In our house, “30 Rock” is the default viewing option when picking something new just feels too taxing.

But the news is supposed to be a little taxing. 

One of my refrains about the news business is that the difference between news and entertainment is that in this work, sometimes you have to tell people what they don’t want to hear.

Of course, that requires an audience willing to listen.

If my colleagues, competitors and I want to claim journalistic privilege, we had better, when necessary, be ready to tell our readers, viewers or listeners things that they won’t like. If we’re hiding or contextualizing into oblivion the important or necessary facts, we’re just content creators looking for some likes.

We can tell it to you gently. We can tell you with respect. But we have to tell you. The thing is, though, you don’t have to listen.

The two things people in our vocation should always strive to provide are, first, reliable information delivered in a timely manner, and second, our judgment about what is and isn’t important. If we’re doing the first part right, we will earn the trust to offer the second. An algorithm can serve up a waterfall of stories arranged to please consumers. A journalist is supposed to work for the chance to be troublesome or difficult, as necessary.

If you’re a regular reader of this column or of The Hill more generally, you can make up your own minds about whether we are living up to the spirit of that charge. But from the inside looking out, I can promise that we are trying every day to live up to the line from Lord Byron once emblazoned on the wall of a newsroom where I learned the trade: “Without, or with, offence to friends or foes, I sketch your world exactly as it goes.” 

I am sometimes failing, sometimes succeeding, but always trying.

So, as this year draws to a close, I ask the same of you. Not as it relates to what I write or what The Hill publishes. If you’ve made it this far in a column about the news business on Christmas week, I assume we already have your indulgence. What I’m challenging you to do in 2026 is push yourself outside the familiar when it comes to the news you use.

Open yourself up to topics and opinions that make you a little uncomfortable. When you’ve reached a conclusion about a controversy, push yourself to find an opposing view — not a straw man ready for torching, but a thoughtful and well-reasoned perspective. Seek out reporting that asks hard questions about the people and causes you are most inclined to support. 

Do this not chiefly to support better journalism — though it will do that. Do this because it will make you a better citizen and a better partner in self-government with your fellow Americans, because you will be better able to see all sides of an issue. 

If we do our jobs and you do yours, wonderful things happen for our country.

Stirewalt is the politics editor for The Hill, veteran campaign and elections journalist and best-selling author of books about American political history.

data.title: Republicans divided on whether Trump should topple Venezuela’s Maduro with military force
data.description:
Republican lawmakers are divided over whether President Trump should escalate military pressure on Venezuela to oust President Nicolás Maduro, with some Republicans warning that “regime change” has a history of backfiring on the United States.

Senate Republicans largely support Trump’s aggressive targeting of Venezuelan speedboats suspected of smuggling drugs, but some warn that attacking Maduro’s regime more directly, either by striking targets on land or putting “boots on the ground,” could go too far.

“I’m certainly following the situation closely, I support what the president’s done. I think the question is how forceful we should do this,” said Sen. Roger Marshall (R-Kan.). “I think we just have to be very careful when we’re dealing with regime change. It seems to backfire a lot.”

Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.) said he believes the administration is pursuing regime change in Venezuela and declared, “I’m opposed to it.”

Paul argued that the Trump is being arbitrary in striking Venezuelan boats suspected of drug smuggling while pardoning the former president of Honduras, Juan Orlando Hernández, who was sentenced to 45 years in prison for partnering with cocaine traffickers to smuggle drugs into the United States.

Trump pardoned Hernández earlier this month.

“They let go of a guy that was in prison for 40 years at a very high level distributing narcotics and then they’re blowing up these other people. It’s the whole danger of what we’ve gotten away from is — at one point in time Congress was supposed to declare war under the Constitution. We’ve gotten away from that,” Paul said.

Paul said that presidents are getting around Congress’s authority to declare war by themselves declaring “war against people we designate to be terrorists.”

“Now they’re designating the government terrorists,” he said.   

Tensions in the region increased over the weekend when the U.S. Coast Guard attempted to intercept an oil tanker headed toward Venezuela to pick up a shipment of crude oil.

U.S. forces have already seized two oil tankers off the coast of Venezuela amid a massive buildup of U.S. naval forces in the Caribbean Sea aimed at putting pressure on Maduro.

A Republican senator who requested anonymity to comment frankly on Trump’s aggressive pressure against Maduro said the Trump administration seems intent on ousting Maduro even though Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth and Secretary of State Marco Rubio told senators in a briefing last week that’s not their goal.

“I do not want to put ground troops in Venezuela, I don’t want to have another Afghanistan or Iraq,” the senator said. “I’m not in favor of U.S.-directed regime change.”

Rubio last week addressed Senate Republicans’ growing concerns that Trump may be planning a more aggressive intervention in Venezuela to oust the Maduro regime, which many Republicans say has held onto power illegitimately.

“Marco said repeatedly that regime change is not the policy of the United States, it was not the focus of the anti-narco-trafficking policy,” the senator said.

Several other senators confirmed that Rubio assured lawmakers on Capitol Hill during a classified briefing that the administration is not pursuing regime change.

But those claims were undercut by comments White House chief of staff Susie Wiles made to journalist Chris Whipple in an interview for Vanity Fair indicating that regime change in Venezuela in Trump’s goal.

“He wants to keep on blowing boats up until Maduro cries uncle,” Wiles said and suggested that if Trump wants to order land strikes he would need Congress’s authority.

Senate Appropriations Committee Chair Susan Collins (R-Maine) says there’s “a lack of clarity” from the administration about its national security goals for the region.

“The briefing … helped somewhat but there’s still a lack of clarity … aside from the drug issue of why we’re involved in this country’s leadership,” she said.

“I don’t have a clear understanding,” she said.

Trump said last month that U.S. strikes against Venezuela could begin “very soon,” a threat that prompted Paul to join a group of Senate Democrats to file a war powers resolution to block U.S. armed forces from engaging in hostilities against Venezuela without authorization from Congress.

Democratic Sens. Tim Kaine (Va.), Adam Schiff (Calif.) and Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.) are leading co-sponsors of the measure with Paul.

Only two House Republicans — Reps. Don Bacon (Neb.) and Thomas Massie (Ky.)  — voted last week in favor of a Democratic resolution to stop hostilities against any presidentially designated terrorist organization in the Western Hemisphere without authorization from Congress.

Some Republican defense hawks have expressed frustration that Rubio and Hegseth haven’t stated more clearly an agenda to force Maduro from power.

“Most Americans want to know what’s gonna happen next. I want to know what’s gonna happen next. Is it the policy to take Maduro down? It should be, if it’s not. And if he goes, what’s gonna happen next? I’d like a better answer as to what happens when Maduro goes,” said Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.), an outspoken advocate for regime change, after meeting with Hegseth and Rubio.

The prospect of expanded military operations against Venezuelan poses thorny questions for MAGA-allied conservatives who have warned against the United States becoming enmeshed in endless wars.

Sen. Cynthia Lummis (R-Wyo.), who considers herself aligned with the America First movement, said she’s usually skeptical of U.S. military interventions aimed at regime change but supports Trump’s pressure on Venezuela to combat drug trafficking.

“I do tend to look askance at more international engagements but I also have a drug-addicted nephew who is in prison and I’ve seen what these drugs are doing to America and it’s terrifying,” she said.

“Maduro is part of that narco-terrorism. I think it’s appropriate to call it narco-terrorism and I’m actually okay with it all,” she said, arguing that Maduro was not legitimately re-elected.

“I don’t know that I would support U.S. boots on the crime to accomplish” regime change “but I’m very hopeful that it will happen regardless,” she said.

Sen. Eric Schmitt (R-Mo.), a staunch Trump ally, said he believes the president is focused on stopping Venezuelan drug-smuggling boats and declined to speculate on whether Maduro needs to be toppled to effectively stop the flow of drugs from his country.  

“Clearly Maduro is part of these cartel rings but right now the focus is to blow the boats that are killing Americans out of the water,” he said.
